scenario: Helios Healthcare embeds the Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot Starter in a service that processes 12000 jobs per day, occasionally with large variable payloads. Before raising the limit in production, the platform team must confirm the documented default of camunda.client.max-message-size on the gRPC channel so it can quantify how much headroom is actually needed.
question: What is the default value of camunda.client.max-message-size in the Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot Starter properties reference?

options.text: 5MB, the maximum inbound message size the client can receive from Camunda, which configures maxInboundMessageSize on the gRPC channel of the client
4MB, the maximum inbound message size the client can receive from Camunda, which configures maxInboundMessageSize on the gRPC channel of the client
16KB, the maximum inbound message size the client can receive from Camunda, which configures maxInboundMessageSize on the gRPC channel of the client
100MB, the maximum inbound message size the client can receive from Camunda, which configures maxInboundMessageSize on the gRPC channel of the client

a.text: Correct. The camunda.client section lists camunda.client.max-message-size with a default value of 5MB. The description specifies that this maxMessageSize sets the maxInboundMessageSize of the gRPC channel for inbound messages from Camunda.

b.text: Incorrect. 4MB is a common gRPC default in other ecosystems but is not the Camunda Spring Boot Starter default; the reference shows 5MB.

c.text: Incorrect. 16KB is the documented default for camunda.client.max-metadata-size (the gRPC metadata limit), not for max-message-size.

d.text: Incorrect. 100MB is the documented default for camunda.client.max-http-connections units context, not max-message-size; the message-size default is 5MB.

explanation: The Spring Boot Starter sets camunda.client.max-message-size to a default of 5MB. This value configures maxInboundMessageSize on the gRPC channel, capping the size of any single inbound message the client accepts from Camunda before raising the limit explicitly.
